title: Unity Interface & Workflow
intro: The Unity Editor is your primary workspace. Mastering the layout, shortcuts, and core concepts like Prefabs and Tags is foundational to every other exam domain.

sections.heading: The Five Core Windows
sections.content:
Unity's default layout consists of five main windows you'll use constantly:

  • Scene View — the interactive 3D/2D viewport for building levels. Use W/E/R for Move/Rotate/Scale, F to focus on a selection.
  • Game View — what the player camera sees. Only active during Play mode, but you can preview it anytime. Never edit the scene while in Play mode unless you copy values out first.
  • Hierarchy — lists every GameObject in the open scene as a parent-child tree. Drag objects onto each other to parent them.
  • Inspector — displays all components and properties of the selected object or asset. This is where you configure everything.
  • Project Window — file browser for your Assets folder. All importable files (scripts, textures, audio, prefabs) live here.
A sixth key window: the Console — shows Debug.Log output, warnings, and errors. Clear it often during testing to catch new messages.

sections.heading: GameObjects and Components
sections.content:
Everything visible (and many invisible things) in a Unity scene is a GameObject. GameObjects themselves do nothing — they are just containers for Components, which provide all behavior and appearance.

  • Every GameObject has a Transform component — position, rotation, scale — which cannot be removed.
  • Components are added via the Inspector (Add Component button) or dragged from the Project window.
  • Scripts are components too — any MonoBehaviour subclass can be added as a component.
  • Parent-child relationships — a child inherits the parent's transform changes. Moving the parent moves all children with it. The child's transform shows its local offset from the parent.

sections.heading: Prefabs
sections.content:
Prefabs are one of Unity's most important features. A Prefab stores a fully configured GameObject (with all its components, child objects, and settings) as a reusable asset in the Project window.

  • Create a Prefab by dragging a GameObject from the Hierarchy into the Project window.
  • All instances placed in scenes are linked to the Prefab. Changes to the Prefab propagate to all instances.
  • Individual instances can have Prefab Overrides — local changes that don't affect other instances or the original.
  • Use Prefab Variants to create specialized versions of a Prefab that inherit from a parent Prefab.
⚡ Exam tip: Prefabs are the answer to 'how do you reuse a configured object in multiple scenes' — always choose Prefab over Template, Prototype, or Script.

sections.heading: Tags and Layers
sections.content:
Tags are string labels assigned to GameObjects. Use them to identify objects in scripts with gameObject.CompareTag("Enemy") or GameObject.FindWithTag("Player").

Layers are integer indices (0-31) used for two main purposes:

  • Camera Culling Mask — Camera only renders objects on selected layers
  • Physics Layer Collision Matrix — control which layers collide with which others

Both Tags and Layers are configured in Project Settings → Tags and Layers, or via the dropdowns in the Inspector.

examTips: Know all Transform tool shortcuts: Q/W/E/R/T/Y
Understand what Prefab Overrides are and when they apply
F = focus on selected object in Scene view
The Hierarchy shows scene objects; Project shows file assets
Tags are for identification; Layers are for rendering and physics filtering

title: C# Scripting in Unity
intro: Scripting is the largest exam domain (~14%). You need to know the MonoBehaviour lifecycle, C# fundamentals, common patterns, and Unity-specific APIs cold.

sections.heading: MonoBehaviour Lifecycle
sections.content:
Every script that inherits from MonoBehaviour has access to lifecycle methods called by Unity's engine. The complete order is:

  1. Awake() — called once when the script loads, even if disabled. Use for self-initialization that doesn't depend on other objects.
  2. OnEnable() — called every time the script is enabled.
  3. Start() — called once before the first Update(), only if enabled. Use for initialization that may reference other objects (they've all had Awake by now).
  4. FixedUpdate() — fixed physics timestep (default 50×/sec). Use for physics forces.
  5. Update() — every frame. Use for input, gameplay logic.
  6. LateUpdate() — every frame, after all Updates. Use for camera logic.
  7. OnDisable() — when the script is disabled or the object is destroyed.
  8. OnDestroy() — just before the object is destroyed.
⚡ Memorize this order cold — it's heavily tested. Key rule: Awake → Start order enables you to safely reference other objects in Start that initialized themselves in Awake.

sections.heading: Essential APIs
sections.content:
These are the Unity C# APIs you'll use in nearly every project:

  • GetComponent<T>() — get a component on this GameObject. Cache results in Awake.
  • Instantiate(prefab, pos, rot) — create a GameObject from a prefab at runtime.
  • Destroy(obj, delay) — remove a GameObject or component.
  • transform.Translate(v) — move relative to local or world space.
  • Time.deltaTime — seconds since last frame. Always multiply movement by this.
  • Input.GetAxis("Horizontal") — float -1 to 1 for smooth analog input.
  • Input.GetKeyDown(KeyCode.Space) — true only on the frame the key is pressed.
  • Debug.Log(message) — print to the Console.

sections.heading: Coroutines
sections.content:
Coroutines are methods that can pause mid-execution and resume later, using the yield return keyword. They run on the main thread but don't block it.

Common yield instructions:

  • yield return null — pause for one frame
  • yield return new WaitForSeconds(2f) — pause for 2 seconds
  • yield return new WaitForFixedUpdate() — pause until next FixedUpdate
  • yield return new WaitUntil(() => condition) — pause until condition is true

Starting and stopping: StartCoroutine(MyMethod()), StopCoroutine("MyMethod").

Coroutines are not multithreading — they run cooperatively on the main thread. Use them for timed sequences, delays, and staged loading — not for CPU-heavy parallel work.

sections.heading: Useful Attributes
sections.content:
C# attributes in Unity modify how scripts and fields behave in the Editor:

  • [SerializeField] — expose a private field in the Inspector without making it public.
  • [HideInInspector] — hide a public field from the Inspector (still serialized).
  • [Header("Movement")] — add a bold header label in the Inspector to organize fields.
  • [Tooltip("Speed in m/s")] — show a tooltip when hovering the field in the Inspector.
  • [Range(0, 10)] — display the field as a slider in the Inspector.
  • [RequireComponent(typeof(Rigidbody))] — auto-add a required component and prevent its removal.
  • [DisallowMultipleComponent] — prevents this component from being added more than once.

examTips: Awake vs Start: use Awake for self-init, Start for cross-object references
Always multiply movement/speed by Time.deltaTime in Update()
FixedUpdate() only — never poll Input in FixedUpdate
[SerializeField] is preferred over public for Inspector exposure
GetComponent returns null if the component doesn't exist — always check
Coroutines use IEnumerator return type, yield return to pause

title: Physics System
intro: Unity has separate physics engines for 3D (PhysX) and 2D (Box2D). You need to know Rigidbodies, Colliders, triggers vs. collisions, raycasting, and forces.

sections.heading: Rigidbody vs Collider
sections.content:
These two components work together but serve different roles:

  • Collider — defines the physical shape of an object for collision detection. Can exist without a Rigidbody (static collider). Types: BoxCollider, SphereCollider, CapsuleCollider, MeshCollider.
  • Rigidbody — hands transform control to the physics engine. Adds mass, drag, gravity, velocity. Receives forces. Without it, a collider is Static.

Object types by component combination:

  • Static — Collider only, no Rigidbody. Immovable (walls, floors). Moving static colliders at runtime is expensive.
  • Rigidbody (Dynamic) — Rigidbody + Collider. Fully physics-simulated.
  • Kinematic — Rigidbody (Is Kinematic = true) + Collider. Moved by script (transform or MovePosition), not forces. Can affect other Rigidbodies.
  • Trigger — Collider with Is Trigger = true. Detects overlaps, no physical response.

sections.heading: Collision Callbacks
sections.content:
Unity calls these methods automatically on scripts when collision events occur:

Physical Collisions (both colliders NOT triggers):

  • OnCollisionEnter(Collision col) — first frame of contact
  • OnCollisionStay(Collision col) — every frame while in contact
  • OnCollisionExit(Collision col) — first frame after contact ends

Trigger Overlaps (at least one Is Trigger):

  • OnTriggerEnter(Collider other)
  • OnTriggerStay(Collider other)
  • OnTriggerExit(Collider other)
⚡ For collision callbacks to fire, at least one object in the pair must have a non-kinematic Rigidbody. Two static colliders touching generate no callbacks.

sections.heading: Forces and ForceMode
sections.content:
Apply forces to a Rigidbody using rb.AddForce(vector, ForceMode):

  • ForceMode.Force (default) — continuous force, affected by mass. N = kg·m/s²
  • ForceMode.Impulse — instant velocity change, affected by mass. Use for jumps, explosions
  • ForceMode.Acceleration — continuous force, ignores mass.
  • ForceMode.VelocityChange — instant velocity change, ignores mass.

Other force-related properties: rb.velocity, rb.angularVelocity, rb.drag, rb.angularDrag, rb.mass.

Setting velocity directly: rb.velocity = new Vector3(x, y, z) — OK for character jumps, but avoid mixing with AddForce.

sections.heading: Raycasting
sections.content:
Raycasting lets you detect objects in a line without physical contact:

RaycastHit hit;
if (Physics.Raycast(origin, direction, out hit, maxDistance))
{
    Debug.Log("Hit: " + hit.collider.name);
    Debug.DrawLine(origin, hit.point, Color.red);
}

Useful overloads:

  • Physics.Raycast(ray, out hit) — using a Ray struct (e.g., Camera.main.ScreenPointToRay(Input.mousePosition))
  • Physics.RaycastAll(origin, dir) — returns all hits, not just the first
  • Physics.SphereCast(), BoxCast() — shaped casts

Use a LayerMask to filter which layers the ray checks against — much faster than hitting everything.

examTips: Rigidbody = physics simulation. Collider = shape. Need both for dynamic physics.
For callbacks to fire: at least one non-kinematic Rigidbody in the pair
Trigger vs Collision: trigger = Is Trigger checked = no physical push
FixedUpdate is where you add forces — never in Update()
ForceMode.Impulse = instant, mass-aware. VelocityChange = instant, massless.
RaycastHit struct holds point, normal, distance, collider, transform

title: Animation System
intro: Unity's Mecanim animation system is built around the Animator Controller state machine. You need to know clips, states, transitions, blend trees, parameters, and events.

sections.heading: Animation Clips and the Animator
sections.content:
An Animation Clip is a recorded timeline of keyframe data — position, rotation, scale, color changes, script variable changes — anything Unity can animate. Clips are assets in the Project window.

The Animator component (on a GameObject) drives animation playback. It requires an Animator Controller asset in its Controller field. The Animator reads the Controller's state machine and plays the appropriate clips.

The Animation window (Window → Animation) lets you record new clips and edit keyframes directly on GameObjects in the scene.

sections.heading: Animator Controller State Machine
sections.content:
The Animator Controller is a visual state machine:

  • States — each state plays one (or a blend of) animation clip(s). The orange state is the default entry state.
  • Transitions — arrows between states with conditions that trigger the switch. Set Has Exit Time to auto-transition when the clip ends, or use parameters for code-triggered transitions.
  • Parameters — variables that scripts can set to drive transitions: Float, Int, Bool, Trigger.

In code: anim.SetFloat("Speed", speed);, anim.SetBool("IsGrounded", true);, anim.SetTrigger("Jump");

A Trigger parameter automatically resets to false after a transition consumes it — unlike Bool, which stays set until you change it.

sections.heading: Blend Trees
sections.content:
A Blend Tree is a special Animator state that blends between multiple clips based on one or two float parameters:

  • 1D Blend Tree — one float parameter (e.g., Speed). Clips at threshold values blend smoothly as the parameter changes.
  • 2D Blend Tree — two float parameters (e.g., X velocity and Z velocity). Clips are placed in 2D space and blended based on the 2D parameter vector.

Common use: idle (0) → walk (0.5) → run (1) all in one Blend Tree controlled by a Speed float. Much cleaner than 3 separate states with transitions between them.

sections.heading: Animation Layers and IK
sections.content:
Animation Layers let you run multiple state machines simultaneously on different body parts. Example: a base layer for locomotion and an upper-body layer for aiming or reloading.

  • Each layer has a weight (0-1) and a mask (Avatar Mask) defining which bones it controls.
  • Blending modes: Override (replaces lower layers) or Additive (adds on top).

Inverse Kinematics (IK) — automatically poses limbs to reach a target position. Requires 'IK Pass' enabled on the layer. Implement in OnAnimatorIK(int layerIndex).

Animation Events — markers on clips that call a named method on the GameObject's scripts. Set via the Animation window or in the Inspector's clip preview.

examTips: Animator component + Animator Controller asset: both required
Triggers auto-reset after use. Bools stay set until you change them.
Blend Trees = smooth blending via float parameter (locomotion is the classic example)
Animation Events call a method by name — method must exist on a script on the same GameObject
Humanoid Avatar enables animation retargeting between different character models

title: Audio System
intro: Unity's audio system is built around three core components: AudioClip (data), AudioSource (emitter), and AudioListener (receiver). Know these well plus the Audio Mixer.

sections.heading: Core Components
sections.content:
The audio pipeline has three players:

  • AudioClip — the audio data asset (imported .wav, .mp3, .ogg). Configured via Inspector: Load Type, Compression Format, 3D/2D.
  • AudioSource — attached to GameObjects to emit sound. Key properties: AudioClip, Volume, Pitch, Loop, Play On Awake, Spatial Blend, Min/Max Distance.
  • AudioListener — the 'ear' of the scene. Exactly one must exist — usually on the Main Camera. Audio is spatialized relative to the listener's position and orientation.

In code: audioSource.Play(), audioSource.Stop(), audioSource.PlayOneShot(clip).

sections.heading: 3D Spatial Audio
sections.content:
Unity simulates 3D audio using the Spatial Blend property on AudioSource:

  • 0 = fully 2D — flat, uniform volume regardless of distance or direction. Good for UI, music, narration.
  • 1 = fully 3D — volume attenuates with distance; panning based on direction relative to listener. Good for in-world SFX.

3D audio is configured with rolloff curves: Linear, Logarithmic, or Custom. The Min Distance defines where max volume starts; Max Distance is where the sound fades to zero.

Audio Reverb Zones add environment-based reverb (cave, hall, underwater) for objects within a defined radius.

sections.heading: Audio Mixer
sections.content:
The Audio Mixer (Window → Audio → Audio Mixer) is a digital mixing board for your game's audio:

  • Groups — create named groups (Master, Music, SFX, Voice). Set each AudioSource's Output to the appropriate group.
  • Effects — add DSP effects to groups: Reverb, Equalizer, Compressor, Echo. Effects applied to a group affect all sources routed through it.
  • Snapshots — saved mixer states you can blend between at runtime. Use for dynamic transitions: underwater → above water, combat music → calm exploration.

Set group volume from code: mixer.SetFloat("MasterVolume", value) where the exposed parameter name matches. Note: Audio Mixer volumes are in decibels (dB) — use Mathf.Log10(sliderValue) * 20 to convert from a 0-1 slider.

examTips: AudioListener = one per scene, on Main Camera. AudioSource = many.
PlayOneShot stacks sound; Play() interrupts itself.
PlayClipAtPoint is perfect for audio on destroyed objects.
Spatial Blend: 0 = 2D, 1 = 3D. Not to be confused with volume.
Audio Mixer volumes are in dB — use the log formula for UI sliders

title: Lighting & Rendering
intro: Lighting defines the visual quality of your game. Understand light types, Materials, Shaders, baking/GI, and the differences between real-time and pre-computed lighting.

sections.heading: Light Types
sections.content:
Unity's four built-in lights (in the Built-in Render Pipeline):

  • Directional Light — casts parallel rays across the entire scene. Position doesn't matter, only rotation. One Directional Light = one shadow map for the whole scene. Simulates sun/moon.
  • Point Light — radiates from a single point in all directions. Has a configurable range. Simulates light bulbs, torches, fires.
  • Spot Light — cone-shaped light. Configurable angle and range. Simulates flashlights, spotlights.
  • Area Light — rectangular soft light. Baked only in Built-in RP (available in URP/HDRP at runtime). Great for windows, studio lighting.

Each light has a Light Mode: Realtime (every frame), Baked (pre-computed), or Mixed (both — direct lighting realtime, indirect baked).

sections.heading: Materials and Shaders
sections.content:
Every rendered surface uses a Material, which is an instance of a Shader:

  • The Shader is a GPU program that calculates pixel color based on lights, textures, and properties. Shaders can be written in HLSL/ShaderLab or created with Shader Graph.
  • The Material stores the property values fed into the Shader: Albedo (base color) texture, Metallic map, Smoothness, Normal map, Emission, etc.

Key Built-in shaders:

  • Standard — PBR shader, metallic workflow. Default for 3D objects.
  • Unlit/Color, Unlit/Texture — not affected by lights at all. Good for UI, flat art, VFX.
  • Sprites/Default — used by Sprite Renderers in 2D.

PBR properties: Metallic (0 = dielectric, 1 = metal), Smoothness (0 = rough/matte, 1 = mirror-like).

sections.heading: Global Illumination and Lightmapping
sections.content:
Global Illumination (GI) simulates indirect light — light that has bounced off surfaces. It creates realistic soft shadows and color bleeding.

  • Baked GI — pre-computed with Unity's Lightmapper (Progressive CPU/GPU). Results stored in Lightmap textures on static objects. Zero runtime cost, but can't change dynamically.
  • Objects must be marked Static → Contribute GI to receive baked lighting.
  • Light Probes — sample points in the scene that store baked lighting for dynamic objects (characters, moving props) that can't use lightmaps.
  • Reflection Probes — capture 360° environment reflections at a point, used for accurate reflections on metallic/glossy surfaces.

Lightmapping is configured in Window → Rendering → Lighting. Click 'Generate Lighting' to bake.

examTips: Directional Light = sun (no position effect). Point = bulb (range limited).
Area Light is bake-only in Built-in RP — remember this for exam questions.
Baked lighting = no runtime cost but no dynamic updates.
Objects must be Static → Contribute GI to be included in lightmap baking.
Light Probes are for dynamic objects that can't use lightmaps.
Standard Shader = PBR. Unlit = ignores lighting entirely.

title: UI Systems
intro: Unity UI (uGUI) is Canvas-based and covers everything from HUDs and menus to in-world UI. You need to know Canvas render modes, RectTransform, EventSystem, and scripting UI.

sections.heading: Canvas and Render Modes
sections.content:
All UI elements must be children of a Canvas GameObject. The Canvas determines how UI is rendered:

  • Screen Space - Overlay — renders directly on the screen surface, always on top of everything. The default. No camera dependency.
  • Screen Space - Camera — rendered by a specified camera at a configurable distance. Allows 3D effects between UI and world objects. Requires a camera reference.
  • World Space — the Canvas exists in 3D world space with a real transform. Occludes/is occluded by 3D objects. Used for in-world panels, enemy health bars, interactive screens.

The Canvas Scaler component controls how UI scales across resolutions. Use 'Scale With Screen Size' with a reference resolution (e.g., 1920×1080).

sections.heading: RectTransform and Anchors
sections.content:
UI elements use a RectTransform instead of a regular Transform. Key properties:

  • Anchors — four normalized points (0-1) on the parent that this element attaches to. Min/Max define a normalized rect on the parent.
  • Anchor Presets — hold Shift/Alt while clicking a preset to also set pivot and position. The preset grid in the Inspector top-left is the fastest way to set up anchors.
  • Pivot — the point the element rotates and scales around (0,0 = bottom-left, 0.5,0.5 = center, 1,1 = top-right).
  • Stretch anchors — set Min=(0,0) and Max=(1,1) to stretch the element to fill its parent. Add Left/Right/Top/Bottom offsets for padding.
⚡ Most resolution issues come from incorrect anchors. Elements anchored to one corner stay near that corner on all screen sizes — not always what you want!

sections.heading: Core UI Components
sections.content:
Key UI components to know:

  • Button — clickable element. OnClick() UnityEvent in Inspector or add listeners in code: button.onClick.AddListener(MyMethod)
  • Image — displays a Sprite. Raycast Target makes it block/pass pointer events.
  • TextMeshProUGUI (TMP) — high-quality text rendering. Change text: tmpText.text = "Score: " + score;
  • Slider — ranged float value. slider.onValueChanged.AddListener(OnSliderChanged)
  • Toggle — checkbox. toggle.isOn
  • InputField (TMP) — text entry. inputField.text
  • Canvas Group — controls alpha, interactability, and raycast for all children at once. Useful for fading whole panels in/out.

sections.heading: EventSystem and Input
sections.content:
The EventSystem is a hidden but critical component in every Unity UI scene. It:

  • Processes pointer events (click, hover, drag) for all Canvas elements
  • Handles keyboard/controller navigation for selectable UI elements
  • Manages which element is currently selected/focused

Without an EventSystem (or if it's disabled), no UI input will work. Unity creates one automatically when you create a Canvas.

For custom pointer interaction on 3D objects (World Space UI), implement the relevant interfaces: IPointerClickHandler, IPointerEnterHandler, etc.

examTips: World Space Canvas is a real 3D object; Overlay always renders on top.
EventSystem is required for all UI input — check if it's missing when UI doesn't respond.
Anchors control scaling behavior — stretch anchors = fill parent.
Raycast Target on an Image blocks pointer events even if it's transparent.
TextMeshPro is preferred over legacy Text for all new projects.
Canvas Scaler → Scale With Screen Size for resolution-independent UI.

title: 2D Game Development
intro: Unity's 2D tools include Sprite Renderer, Rigidbody2D, the Tilemap system, and the 2D Physics engine (Box2D). Key differences from 3D are important to know.

sections.heading: Sprites and Rendering
sections.content:
Sprites are 2D graphics — imported textures set to Texture Type: Sprite (2D and UI) in the Inspector. The Sprite Editor lets you slice a sprite sheet into individual sprites.

The Sprite Renderer component displays a sprite in the scene. Key properties:

  • Sprite — the sprite asset to display.
  • Color — tint the sprite (white = original color).
  • Flip X/Y — mirror the sprite without scaling by -1.
  • Sorting Layer and Order in Layer — control draw order.
  • Draw Mode — Simple, Sliced (9-slice), or Tiled.

Sorting Layers (named layers, ordered by list) take priority. Within a layer, Order in Layer (integer) controls front-to-back order.

sections.heading: 2D Physics
sections.content:
Unity uses Box2D for 2D physics — a separate engine from 3D PhysX. Key 2D components:

  • Rigidbody2D — 2D physics body. Has Body Type: Dynamic, Kinematic, or Static. Gravity Scale multiplies global gravity.
  • Collider2D variants: BoxCollider2D, CircleCollider2D, CapsuleCollider2D, PolygonCollider2D, EdgeCollider2D, CompositeCollider2D.
  • PhysicsMaterial2D — sets friction and bounciness for 2D colliders.

2D collision callbacks work the same as 3D: OnCollisionEnter2D(Collision2D col), OnTriggerEnter2D(Collider2D other). Note the 2D suffix — 3D and 2D physics callbacks are separate methods.

2D force API: rb2d.AddForce(Vector2 force), rb2d.velocity, rb2d.MovePosition().

sections.heading: Tilemap System
sections.content:
The Tilemap system is Unity's solution for efficient 2D tile-based levels:

  • Grid — parent GameObject defining the cell size and layout.
  • Tilemap — child of Grid. Holds the tile data (which tile at which grid coordinate).
  • Tilemap Renderer — renders the Tilemap visually.
  • Tile Palette (Window → 2D → Tile Palette) — the painting tool for placing tiles.
  • Tile assets — each unique tile is a .asset file referencing a sprite. Rule Tiles auto-select sprites based on neighboring tiles (great for autotiling).

For physics on tilemaps: add Tilemap Collider 2D and optionally a Composite Collider 2D (which merges individual tile colliders for massive performance gains).

examTips: 2D physics uses Box2D; 3D uses PhysX — separate systems, separate components
Use the 2D suffix for callbacks: OnCollisionEnter2D, OnTriggerEnter2D
Sprite Atlas reduces draw calls by batching sprites from the same texture
Sorting Layer first, then Order in Layer for draw order control
Composite Collider 2D merges Tilemap tile colliders — always use it for large tilemaps
Rigidbody2D Gravity Scale = 0 makes an object weightless

title: Project Management & Build
intro: The exam covers the project pipeline from setup and organization to building and deployment. Know the Package Manager, Build Settings, Profiler, and version control best practices.

sections.heading: Project Organization
sections.content:
Good project structure prevents issues as projects grow:

  • Organize the Assets folder by type: Scripts/, Prefabs/, Sprites/, Audio/, Scenes/, Materials/.
  • Use descriptive, consistent naming (e.g., Player_Idle_Clip, EnemySpawner_Prefab).
  • Avoid spaces in file/folder names — can cause issues on some platforms.

Version Control (Git):

  • Track the Assets/ and ProjectSettings/ folders.
  • Ignore Library/, Temp/, Builds/, obj/ — these are auto-generated.
  • Always commit .meta files — they store GUIDs and import settings. Losing them breaks all asset references.

sections.heading: Package Manager and Asset Store
sections.content:
The Package Manager (Window → Package Manager) is the central hub for extending Unity:

  • Unity Registry — official Unity packages (Input System, TextMeshPro, Cinemachine, URP, ProBuilder, etc.).
  • My Assets — your Asset Store purchases.
  • In Project — packages currently installed.
  • Git URL / Local — install packages from a GitHub URL or local disk path.

Key packages to know: TextMeshPro, Input System, Universal Render Pipeline (URP), Cinemachine, 2D Sprite.

sections.heading: Build Settings and Deployment
sections.content:
File → Build Settings is the gateway to deploying your game:

  • Scenes in Build — drag scenes from the Project window. The scene at index 0 is the startup scene.
  • Platform — select target: PC (Windows/Mac/Linux), WebGL, Android, iOS, etc. Install platform modules via Unity Hub.
  • Player Settings — accessed from Build Settings. Configure: Company/Product Name, app icon, resolution, bundle ID, rendering settings, quality settings per platform.

After building, Unity outputs a standalone executable (PC), .apk/.aab (Android), or web build folder (WebGL) depending on the target platform.

sections.heading: Performance and Debugging Tools
sections.content:
Profiler (Window → Analysis → Profiler):

  • Records CPU, GPU, Memory, Rendering, Physics, and Audio data per frame.
  • Use the CPU Usage module to find expensive function calls.
  • Profile a Device Build for accurate mobile performance data.

Frame Debugger (Window → Analysis → Frame Debugger):

  • Steps through individual draw calls for a single frame — helps identify overdraw, incorrect rendering order, and shader issues.

Debug tools in code:

  • Debug.Log(), Debug.LogWarning(), Debug.LogError()
  • Debug.DrawLine(start, end, color) — visible only in Scene view
  • Debug.DrawRay(origin, dir, color)
  • Debug.Break() — pauses Play mode

examTips: Build Settings: scenes index 0 = startup scene
.meta files must be committed to version control — they hold GUIDs
Profiler = performance data. Frame Debugger = draw calls per frame.
Player Settings is accessed via Build Settings, not Project Settings directly
Unity ignores Library/ and Temp/ — never commit them to Git
Know the key packages: TMP, Input System, URP, Cinemachine
